nativeRange.habitat: The Red-lined Torpedo Barb originates from fast-flowing, well-oxygenated hill streams and rivers in the Western Ghats region of southwestern India, where the water runs clear and cool over rocky substrate with moderate to strong current.

decorPreferences: smooth rounded boulders and cobblestones replicating hill-stream substrate
strong filtration with powerheads to simulate current
open midwater swimming space in the center
robust plants (Java fern, Bolbitis, Anubias) anchored to rocks or driftwood
dimmer lighting or floating plants to reduce glare

diet.notes: The Red-lined Torpedo Barb accepts a wide variety of prepared foods, with high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and sinking pellets forming a good staple. These should be supplemented regularly with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, and mosquito larvae. Blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach) and spirulina-based flake provide beneficial plant matter. In the wild, the species grazes algae and biofilm from rocks as well as small invertebrates. Small amounts offered two to three times daily suit the high activity level of this species well.

goodWith: other large peaceful cyprinids (e.g., giant danios, larger barbs)
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish (active swimmers may stress them)
small nano fish that may be outcompeted at feeding
delicate or fragile species sensitive to current
fin-nipping targets with flowing fins such as bettas or fancy guppies

imageCandidates.notes: Anandarajkumar; 2880x1941. Sharp, vibrant school of 6+ adults against a dark planted background, each showing the scarlet lateral stripe, black stripe and black-and-yellow caudal markings; best represents this shoaling species.
H. Zell; 5166x3060. Tack-sharp full side profile of a single large adult; clearly shows the red lateral stripe, black stripe, red dorsal-fin tip and the yellow-and-black caudal lobes.
Anandarajkumar; 2499x1656. Clean, crisp lateral profile against green plants with well-saturated diagnostic coloration; complementary angle to the other picks.

summary: The Red-lined Torpedo Barb is a striking, torpedo-bodied cyprinid endemic to the Western Ghats of Kerala, India. Its vivid scarlet lateral stripe and bold black-and-yellow caudal fin have made it highly sought-after in the hobby, contributing to its IUCN Endangered status. Best kept in groups of five or more in a large, well-oxygenated aquarium with strong water flow.
careNotes: The Red-lined Torpedo Barb — known in the scientific literature as Dawkinsia denisonii, with the older trade name Puntius denisonii still appearing on some labels — is a fast, active mid-water shoaler that demands substantial horizontal swimming space. A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is recommended for a group of five adults; larger is strongly preferred. Filtration must deliver strong, well-oxygenated flow — powerheads or a sump return directed along the tank length help simulate the hill-stream current these fish experience in the wild. Temperature tolerance is notably cool for a tropical fish (18–26°C), and sustained temperatures above 26°C are stressful; this also means the species tends to do well in homes where tropical heating may be inconsistent during cooler months. Maintain pH 6.5–7.8 and moderate hardness. Water quality must be excellent: the species is sensitive to elevated nitrates and dissolved organic load, so regular large water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential. Groups of at least five to seven are recommended to distribute any minor social tension and to elicit the natural shoaling behaviour that shows off the species’ best colours. Solitary individuals become reclusive and pale. D. denisonii is listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List, largely due to overcollection for the ornamental trade; hobbyists are generally advised to seek tank-bred specimens where available. Aquacultured individuals are now produced in Southeast Asia and tend to adapt more readily to aquarium conditions than wild-caught fish. The species is generally not fin-nippy despite its active nature, and it will typically coexist peacefully with robust tankmates of similar size. It will jump if startled — a tight-fitting lid is essential.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Red-lined Torpedo Barb is rare and considered difficult. Commercial aquaculture has been achieved using hormone-induced spawning protocols, primarily in Southeast Asian fish farms, and most specimens available in the hobby today originate from these operations. In home aquaria, successful spawning has been reported only occasionally. The species is an egg-scatterer that shows no parental care, and eggs are prone to fungal infection. Conditioning attempts in a dedicated species tank may involve soft, slightly acidic water, regular live-food feeding, and a gradual reduction in temperature toward the lower end of the range (around 18–20°C) to simulate seasonal changes. Adults should be removed after spawning, as they will consume the eggs and fry. Fry are very small and considered challenging to raise.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dawkinsia denisonii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided scientific name, taxonomy, native range (Western Ghats, Kerala/Karnataka), water parameters (temperature 15–22°C per wild data, recommended aquarium 18–26°C), pH, hardness, adult size (to 15 cm), habitat description, diet, breeding notes, and compatibility guidance. Reclassification history (Puntius → Sahyadria → Dawkinsia, Pethiyagoda et al. 2012) documented.

additional.name: FishBase — Dawkinsia denisonii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max TL ~15 cm), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Western Ghats river systems of Kerala and Karnataka), temperature range, and IUCN Endangered listing. Confirmed fast-water hill stream habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Denison Barb Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided trade-focused care guidance: group size (5+), tank size (55–125 gal), temperature 60–75°F (15.5–24°C), pH 6.5–7.8, diet (omnivore — flake, frozen foods, vegetables), jump risk (tight lid required), and note on aquacultured availability improving. Confirmed intermediate care level and shoaling requirement.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Denison’s Barb (Dawkinsia denisonii)
additional.notes: Provided background on IUCN Endangered status, collection pressure history, aquaculture developments, and husbandry detail including water flow requirements and tank footprint recommendations.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Dawkinsia denisonii
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Endangered (EN) status, endemic range restricted to the Western Ghats (Kerala and parts of Karnataka), and primary threat as overcollection for the ornamental aquarium trade. Also notes habitat degradation as a contributing factor.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is an egg-scatterer that shows no parental care. Captive spawning without hormone induction is rarely achieved, and commercial aquaculture using hormone protocols represents the main trade source. Home breeding attempts are generally conducted in a dedicated tank with cool conditioning temperatures (around 18–20°C), soft slightly acidic water, and regular live-food feeding. Eggs are prone to fungal infection, and adults will consume both eggs and fry if not removed promptly after spawning.
